have consider this i resolve , that i can not spend my time more manly , and philosophical than in a enquiry , what the happiness of man be , and how attainable : every advance towards this be a accession to my life and be ; and all travail which do not lead i on towards this end be but so much of life misspend and lose ; what a silliness be it to load my memory with term and word , with numerous instance of matter of fact , to marshal up in order , line , and figure , to talk of unknown sea and distant shore , to tumble over each page in nature Systeme ? what trifle cunning to skill the gainful mystery of trade ? what solemn and laborious foppery to penetrate into all the subtlety of government and art of conversation ? if after all i have no receipt for a trouble mind , no cure for distemper passion , if i have no principle to support my mind under a sink fortune , or govern it in a rise one , if i have nothing to arm i against my fear , or to disperse my grief ; will any one think i have spend my time well , or stock my self with useful knowledge ? but to find out what will make i happy , to find out what will free my mind from the slavery of uneasy passion , what will make it serene , steady , great and manly in all the accident of life ; this every man see at the first blush to be a wise , generous and serviceable employment of my reason . This , aequè pauperibus prode , locupletibus aequè , aequè neglectum pveris senibus { que } nocebit . this learning rich and poor alike do need , and its neglect do|his certain ruin breed , to old and young alike — This occasion saint Austin be ingenious reflection on the poly-theism of the Romans ; when he consider that they worship Felicitas ( by which they mean that deity that can confer happiness upon man ) he can not but wonder , why this be not the most ancient of all their God ; or why , when this deity be find out , they do not present discard the other idle multitude of superfluous and unnecessary god ; since this alone be sufficient to supply all the necessity , and gratify all the desire and appetite of mankind . but though happiness shall be a project too great , the necessity of this enquiry for the redress of humane misery . too ambitious for a poor silly mortal ; yet sure the redress of those evil which oppress our state and nature be such a humble and modest design as may well become the meanness of man ; and therefore if i can not excuse the confidence or presumption of this enquiry by plead the innate desire of happiness ; This therefore must be the supposition of my follow discourse . there may be three different kind of incapacity fancy , which for distinction sake I|will call natural , moral , and penal : each of which may be thus explain in the notion the objector form of they : penal incapacity be that desperate state wherein man be by God immutable reject both from pardon and assistance . moral incapacity result from the strength and absoluteness of that dominion which sin have establish over some man , through a long and continue course of wickedness . natural incapacity consist in such a inteachable stupidity of temper , or in such violent and invincible inclination to vice , or in such a slightness , levity and inconstancy of mind , as render man utter unfit to receive any last impression of virtue , or to make any steady and resolute attempt of attain it . no incapacity from nature . I|will begin with this first , and here i desire to be excuse if i do not take upon i to mark out the distinct bound and limit of nature and grace ; these two sister be not like those Chaldee brethren Gen. 13. Abraham and lot , that be too mighty to dwell together . no , they delight to mix in love embrace , their wealth and power increase by be unite ; and like some plant i have read of , they never thrive when divide : i shall not dispute , what power in man be a birthright , what a donative ; for alas ! Every thing he possess be a grace , a favour of his prince : his natural ability be so many grace he derive from God , and as proper such as any accession to they which be inspire afterward : so that when ever i contend that any thing be in the power of man , i desire to be understand of all that power which God have invest he with , whether natural or supernatural . do man decry and vilify nature , to beget in themselves the more profound humility , and the more wakeful and solicitous industry , do they like the Semnones , in morib . German . Tacitus , load themselves with chain as the badge of their subjection to and dependence upon the deity , do they magnify divine grace , in order to convince themselves of the necessity and efficacy of it , and so to inflame their importunity and industry in quest of it ; this be piety and devotion , not error ; or however they may exaggerate the impotence of nature beyond strict truth , yet this will be a safe and pious error , as all humble and modest one be : but when they endeavour to represent nature vile and corrupt , on purpose that they may the more licentious pollute and abuse it , when they magnify and exalt divine grace out of a most contradictious and preposterous design to justify their neglect and contempt of it , for they will fain have all to be so entire impute to grace , that they will not themselves be put to as much as the trouble of seek it , it|be not only a error , but a pernicious and fatal one : for he that abandon the use of his reason , render himself incapable of any heavenly aid ; God give his grace to man , not beast ; i must therefore oppose this fancy and endeavour to persuade man , that it be in their power to be virtuous and happy : nor can i think this assertion any way injurious to the honour or goodness of God , if it be remember , that whatever power i attribute to man , i acknowledge derive from God . i will therefore with good assurance proceed and try whether i can take in and demolish this fort which stop our way to happiness . they who affirm a great part of mankind incapable of virtue , forget that they dishonour God while they reproach their nature : for be it so , to what end can we imagine such man endow with reason and understanding ? not to worship but defy their maker ? and be it for this end that they be make immortal too ? have God make man only to take his pastime in the world , like the Leviathan in the wave , such a soul as that which move the fish of the sea , or the beast of the earth , a sensitive soul have be most proper for this end ; then may he have enjoy himself without reluctancy , without control , without remorse , without shame : what can be the proper work of a rational creature , to which you allow not a capacity of virtue and religion ? till you can show i this , i can never believe that God shove endow man with a rational and immortal mind out of any other design than such a one as may become such a be create after his own image , which be the practice of holiness and virtue . but what shove i wonder that man shove not be aware of their contradict reason when they seem to be insensible of that contradiction even to the common sense and experience of mankind which they be guilty of ? to what purpose be there so many school of learning and good manner found ? to what purpose be there so many treatise of the education of youth write ? to what purpose do the wakeful parent strive to inculcate the seed of virtue into the child , and train he up by a wise discipline to the practice and custom of virtue ? to what purpose be the proposal of reward and punishment , and the restraint of law , if either they do not raise those hope and fear they aim at , or if hope and fear be altogether useless and ineffectual , if no instruction no discipline can mould and fashion rough , unpolished , crooked , incorrigible nature ? now here though any man may have confidence enough to disparage the judgement of mankind , and attribute all the pain they take in the education of youth , or the government and direction of ripe year , to custom not to right reason ; yet sure he will not so far disparage his own observation and knowledge as utter to deny the success of these mean ; for not to instance in particular , it|be not unknown to any one the least verse in the history of the world , that there have be national virtue as well as vice , that there have be time wherein learning and religion have be as much in fashion and reputation , as wickedness and barbarism in other , shall we say , those nation , those time breed none of those nature , which the objector affirm be uncapable of virtue ? let they show what heavenly influence , what miraculous power produce this change in nature : what shove i urge the power of vain and false principle , the mighty force even of irrational custom , vanquish those inclination which be more deep root , more close interweave with our blood and spirit than any inclination to vice and folly can be ? such be for example the love of life , and the Abhorrence of pain ; and yet what a contempt of death be to be find even in the most timorous sex , as in the indian woman ! what a contempt of pain even in the weak age , as in the spartan youth ! and all this have no strong foundation than irrational custom , and vain fantastic principle : why shove we therefore be unwilling to attribute to excellent principle and virtuous custom , bless and aid by heaven , as much power and virtue as we do to such as these ? if the natural tendency of man to wickedness can be curb , if his most furious and violent passion can be restrain and stifle , than i think , it may be as reasonable suppose that divine truth , religious discipline , together with the grace of God may effect this , as any thing else whatever . if the press necessity and perplexity of the state can change the softness and luxury of Otho into military hardship and courage ; i know not why a rational sense of the true honour and glory of virtue , and of our eternal interest , and innumerable other consideration which the gospel lay before we Christians , shove not be able to work the same wonder ? if the reverence of Seneca or the senate , or any other motive can produce a Quinquennium Neronis , can restrain the violent inclination of that wretched man , so that his government for so many year , shall be as gentle and just as that of the most gracious and virtuous prince , why shall not the reverence of God and the terror of eternity be able to awe and curb the most vicious nature ? This methinks aught well to be weigh by all who assert man impotence and incapacity of virtue , they disparage the gospel and reproach grace as well as man with impotence and insufficiency ; and yet both the one and the other be the power of God , and that in order to salvation : do you consider , that if you suppose man by nature able to do any thing that be good , and then deny he , and utter debar he from God grace , you introduce fate ? for what more fatal necessity can a wretched creature lie under than natural impotence utter destitute and for ever forsake of divine assistance ? or if you bereave not man of grace , but yet bereave grace of its sufficiency , do you not understand that the fatal necessity continue still the same ? there be no change in the man condition , only in this hypothesis , grace be dishonour and reproach as well as nature : and this reflect very rude too upon God , it call the wisdom , the goodness , the sincerity , the power of God into dispute : it|be inconsistent with the power of God not to be able by his spirit and truth to subdue and overpower the corruption of nature ; it|be inconsistent with his goodness , not to be willing to aid his poor creature , when they call upon he ; inconsistent with his sincerity to afford they such aid as must tend to their great mischief not good , as grace its self will , if it be only sufficient to increase their gild , but not to subdue their sin : this be indeed when a child ask bread to give he a stone , Matth. 7. and when he ask fish to gie he a scorpion . it|be last , as inconsistent with the wisdom of God to confer grace to no purpose , as it be with his goodness to confer it to a ill one . the instruction of youth in christian piety . part V. of the choice of a state of life . this instruction will be imperfect , and destitute of one of the best and most necessary part , if after have show how we must live during youth , it shall not direct how to make a good choice of the condition or state wherein one ought to pass the remainder of his life . this choice be a subject , who knowledge be by so much more necessary to young person , as its importance be unknown to the great part of man , and the fault that be there commit be most common irreparable ; or if sometime they be repair , it be with very great pain and difficulty . beside , they be not light or of small consequence , because the issue of they be extend to all the life of a man , and pass even to his eternal salvation , of which they often draw the ruin after they . for this reason , Theotime , i beseech you read attentive this last part , whether before you enter upon this deliberation , or when you shall be at the point of make your choice , and also after you have make it ; for you will find therein whereby to profit at each of those time . CHAP. i how important it be to make a good choice of a state of life . this importance be build upon two truth , two fundamental truth in this matter . which be to be suppose here as fundamental in this matter . the first be , that although all state may be good , yet all state be not good for every one ; and that such a state be profitable for one man which will be hurtful for another , all not have the same inclination , nor then same capacity , nor then same favour of God . the second be , that God who have establish by his providence the diversity of state and employment of the life of man , distribute they different by his wisdom , design some for one employment , other for another : as a father of a family divide among his domestic the office of his house , accord as he find they fit . for this reason he give to man different inclination , diverse natural ability , as well corporal as spiritual ; and also distribute among they his favour diverse , accord to the several necessity of different state , to which he call they . these two truth thus presuppose , evident manifest how important it be to choose well one state and condition : for if all state be not good for all , it be then most important to choose advise , that we may not fall into a state which may be contrary to we . and if God call each man to one state more particular than another , it follow that we must proceed therein with a great circumspection , to choose what be most conformable to his will , and for which he have give we most capacity , and prepare most favour . this choice be of such consequence , how important the choice of a state be . that on it depend all the good of a man , both for this life , and for eternal salvation . be attentive , Theotime , to comprehend the mischief into which all ill choice of a condition cast man , and into which it will cast you , if you be defective therein . first , 1. for the present life . for the present life , what good , what contentment can he have , who be enter into a state which he have ill choose , and for which he be not proper ? there be no condition more miserable . the displeasure of see himself engage against his inclination , join with the difficulty he undergo to acquit himself of his duty , cast he into a disquiet and melancholy , which incessant gnaw he , make he insupportable to other , and himself , and find in his condition a more horrid prison than that of criminal , and chain more unmerciful than those of galleyslave . as for salvation , 2. for salvation . what can a man do in that state ? for beside that melancholy cast he into a continual idleness , and that idleness into vice and perdition ; with what trouble must he effect his salvation in a state wherein he have neither ability , nor vocation from God ? the want of these two thing be the cause why he will commit a infinite number of sin , which he will not have do in another state . the defect of capacity make he find continual difficulty in satisfy his duty , and the particular obligation of his state . the defect of vocation be the cause why he want many favour he shall have receive in another call , and of which he be make unworthy , be enter rash into this condition , without consult God , without demand of he his will . and certain , if we search into the cause of the disorder we see in each state , ecclesiastical , religious , and secular , wherein many discharge themselves of their duty very bad , we shall find the great part of the mischief spring from this source , that be , from a ill entrance into a state of life ; and that the great part enter upon slight ground , without examine whether they be proper for it , and call by God . for a conclusion of this importance , a profitable advice . i exhort all those who deliberate about the choice of their state , attentive to reflect upon these three thing . 1. upon the displeasure and sadness they will feel all their life , for have make a ill choice of their call . 2. the great number of sin they will commit in a state they have light choose , and which they will not have commit in another condition . 3. the danger to which they expose their salvation , in choose after this manner . they who will attentive consider these three thing , will take care not to be defective in a affair of this importance . CHAP. ii of the fault that be ordinary commit in this choice . i find four sort of fault which young man be accustom to fall into , four ordinary fault in this choice . in this so important a choice , which be the cause why they succeed therein so very bad . first , 1. they deliberate not . they deliberate not at all upon this choice , and instead of choose a state accord to the rule of reason , and after a long and serious deliberation , they engage themselves in a call sometime by a sudden passion and fancy , sometime by occasion , and frequent by the sole inclination they feel to one condition rather than another , without examine whether they be proper for it , whether it be for their good , and whether they shall effect therein their salvation . second , when they deliberate , 2. they deliberate ill . they propose thing naughty , that be , upon ill ground , and regard other end than such as they ought to have before their eye in this deliberation . some look upon the sweetness of life in the state they will embrace ; or that positive conclusion , Psal. 97.4 . the lord shall not see , neither shall the god of Jacob regard . but what say the psalmist to these brutish so•s ? he that plant the ear , shall he not hear ? he that form the eye , shall he not see , ver . 9 let these atheist know that God see , and set down all their secret wickedness , and will bring it forth before angel and man at the great day of reckon : the sin of Judah be write with a pen of iron , Jer. 17.1 . and with a point of a diamond , it can never be raze out but by the blood of Christ ; and though , by multiply act of notorious sin , some may blur the engraving of sin on the table of their heart , yet it shall be as write with the juice of Lemmons , be hold to the fire of God wrath , it|be as legible to the conscience as the first moment when the sin be commit : oh the secret wickedness that wicked man have to reckon for ! but where be the secret prayer ?
